video_title: Lawfare Daily: Lies, Laws, and Campaigns
transcript: We are here today with Andrew Weissman who is an accomplished professor of law at New York University and a well-known commentator on MS Now for legal and prosecutorial matters but more important for the purposes of this podcast has also occupied a wide slate of positions at both the main justice department and the FBI. He worked in the US attorney's office for the Eastern District of New York. He worked at main justice in the fraud section and he was also the general counsel of the FBI. So he brings a wide variety of experience to the topics we are going to discuss today. I want to open our conversation with a sort of inquiry about why this book is coming out now. And the answer is a little bit self-evident from the title. But the problem of politicians lying to the public is really quite evergreen. I think if we go back to Homer, we would find examples of it there. And it's certainly something that any historian of the LA latter-day United States would be familiar with. So why is this moment of deceit in politics different from those that have occurred in the past? >> Um well one thank you for having me. you know, when I was at the bureau uh and was the general counsel, I used to religiously read lawfare just like every single day because they just had such a great um compilation of people and issues and it was really smart and um I think it's done a great job of speaking to multiple audiences um both people sort of in the field and then also lay people and if anything it's gotten better um and wider in terms of what it does. So any that's a long way of saying thank you so much um for having me um and it's great having this conversation with you um given our FBI overlap um in in terms of our our work experience. Um so uh so uh so the the book is Liars's Kingdom and so as you're you're correct uh and I point out in the book that um this is not a unique time. It's not like we don't have substantial historical uh examples that we can point to. Um and you know there's obviously Mark Twain you know who commented um you know very famously and quipped about um political lies. And so on the one hand I do think it is a perennial problem and it's one that I think other times in our history and other countries and civilizations have had to deal with. Having said that, um I don't think in my lifetime that we've had such brazeness about lies and ones that are so readily accepted by an inordinate uh number of people. So, let me just explain really quickly what I mean by that, which is so one example of, you know, a lie that that I sort of take throughout this book is the big lie about the 2020 election that there was material fraud. Um, I obviously can't cover every every lie told by Donald Trump or every politician. The Washington Post catalog just in the first term that there were 30,000 false or misleading statements. So I didn't want to write a encyclopedia. I wanted to write something that people could read. So I took that as the core lie. Um polling on that which has been incredibly consistent are that twothirds of Republicans believe that and a third of the electorate believes that lie. And I think part of that is because we are in a substantially different media environment than we have been in certainly since um Oliver Wendell Holmes talked about sort of the marketplace of ideas. I mean he didn't actually use that exact phrase but that was sort of his concept. Um, and because of this bulcanization of the media, I think that lies can be much more pernitious and prevalent because the whole concept that they would rub up against the truth and be combed by the truth and justice is saying um, you know, the remedy for false speech is true speech. That presupposes that they're going to be heard. Um, and with our current media environment, which I don't see getting any better, that precondition for a marketplace of ideas is no longer there in the way that it used to be. >> Okay. So, you mentioned Wendell Holmes's famous well his statements which later got turned into raise the marketplace of ideas. >> Exactly. And that was in the context of first amendment juristprudence. And your book is very much a dialogue, at least I felt, with traditional conceptions, by which I mean ones that have gained currency in modern court decisions of the First Amendment. And you lay out a proposal that by your own admission is going to get some push back from people who are really wedded to the marketplace of ideas theory. Can you sort of just give us a brief overview of what your proposed solutions to this problem are? I think by the end of the book you lay out sort of three variations on a similar >> Yeah. So, um, maybe just turning to sort of the possible solutions at either the federal or state or both level because this could all happen uh in either one. And, you know, to be candid, it's much more likely if it's going to happen at a state level first, certainly right now where, you know, we have a a Congress that doesn't seem to be able to, you know, do do much of anything. So I I think you can uh bifurcate sort of the proposals into sort of two camps. One is um simply criminalizing an intentionally false statement. Uh and you know I can talk about that ad nauseium and the other is a disqualification model. Um and for both of those I go through sort of what the pros and cons are, what the um various precedents that you might have. But those are sort of the two ideas that one is if you tell an intentional lie of some in some material way, you could be prosecuted or um you could be um you could be disqualified as a candidate. An example of the latter would be England. England has a law that if you lie about a political opponent, you can be disqualified. to be fair, they don't enforce it all that often and it may be that they don't have to do it that much. Um or and they're much more judicious about when they do it. And then you have um uh the other which is to criminalize an intentionally false speech. Um and one easy way to think about that in terms of um the sort of why it would make sense is right now in this country if you um are called to testify before Congress and you lie that is criminalized. Roger Stone um was convicted of that, charged, had a trial, had due process, convicted. But if Congress lies to us, there no legal ramifications. Um, and you know, there may there may be political ramifications, but um, there's certainly no legal ramifications for that. Um, and that's sort of the main theme of the book, which is why is that? Um, and looking at that particular issue. >> Okay. So there's a lot to unpack here because this has a lot of both immediate consequences but also those of the third and second order category and we can do it through a largely theoretical model or through sort of pragmatic questions that naturally follow. I'd like to do both but I will give you um a sort of dealer's choice on which one we tackle first. >> Well yeah I'm in your hands. >> Okay let let's start with the practical ones. You are no stranger by any means to going after politicians and their immediate circle for a wide variety of criminal acts. And your background is primarily as a prosecutor. Mine's primarily as an investigator. But we both know, I think, that lies rarely occur by themselves. They are often attempts to obfiscate other crimes or even when they're not, they're usually occurring from the same sort of nexus of facts that lead to other criminal behavior. So I I suspect the answer to this is going to be pretty obvious, but I want to hear it from you since you have the firsthand experience. Why is our existing prosecutorial and judicial system not enough to cover the waterfront on these issues? Because if we go after somebody primarily for fraud, primarily for white collar crimes, primarily um for process or substantive crimes, like we don't have to implicate First Amendment concerns in a way that your proposals might. So, and I realize this is just sort of me rephrasing our first question, but why do we need to change now? >> So, you know, I think that when it comes to politicians that lie, I'm not sure I agree that that the lie is one that um goes with other prosecutable crimes. Um and uh I also feel like the reason that we have both civil and criminal laws with respect to false intentionally false statements, whether it's defamation law, whether it's lying to Congress, whether it's lying to banks, whether it's lying to shareholders, whether it's lying to an FBI agent, um or a prosecutor, um is to have an effect on people's conduct. So they think long and hard as you know um before an interview if you if you and I were doing an interview together um I would start by saying to the person who was being interviewed usually their lawyer would be there and I'd introduce all of us and I'd say before we begin I want to make sure you understand both your rights and your obligations. And when it got to obligations, I would go through as I I just had a I used to say exactly the same thing to everyone about what is required about telling the truth and what the consequences could be if you do not tell the truth. Now, it could be a little scary for people to hear that, but that wasn't really the point. It was to make sure they understood what they were getting into. And ideally for the the there's like a bell curve of people. There are those people who are going to tell the truth no matter what and there probably are those people who are going to lie no matter what. But you want the whole point of having some either criminal or civil strictures is to for those people who will change their behavior based on knowing what the law is. Think of speeding in a car and seeing a you know seeing a police uh car uh as you're driving by. you slow down. And so, um, I think there's an important effect of having a law where people think twice before they say something, um, to make sure that it is accurate. This is something that you know you and I are sort of still relatively new to sort of the public speaking quasi journalist role that we take and that you know but you and I both now comment and give give our analysis on things and and are very much exposed to people who are real journalists on a day in and dayout basis and as you know there's standards departments for any reputable journalism um organization to make sure that what is being said is accurate and sourced, but also they are concerned about making sure there isn't a defamation lawsuit. And to me, if you do not have that, the you know, for the the bad actor and the people who could be persuaded, you are saying, well, you know what, there really isn't there there may be a a political deterrent and that may be less and less. And by the way, it may come after you've already won the election. And so, you know, you you may get the benefits of your lie, but you want to have that deterrent at the time so that they are that squashed because we're owed we're owed that as the people who these people are going to represent. >> Yeah. what you're actually talking about is very similar to the change in footing the FBI took um while we were both there where it went from investigating things almost entirely after the fact to where it really became an organization that was based on preventing harm in the first place. And I think what you're getting at is that in today's sort of what you described as a skewed marketplace because of the bulcanization of media and the myriad voices proliferating um our legal regime has to be more focused on preventing the lies from circulating in the first place because once they're in the ether they're out of control versus going after somebody after the much better said than than much much better and much faster. So, >> well, it's something we've both been thinking about um since roughly 2016, I suspect. >> So, all right. So, there's I'm curious about this though because what you are talking about is it's an imposition. I I I think it's a a pretty modest one, but it does require a reconceptualization of the First Amendment. Um, and we live in a world, and this speaks to your comments about bulanization. We live in a world where an airhead who happens to have a we the people tattoo is given as much credibility as John Hart Eli or Irwin Chemarinsky when it comes to expounding on the Constitution. So, I'm going to give you a softball and just ask you to defend. Why is this imposition, which some people um will no doubt view as very extreme, actually not that out of the main discourse of American history? So, um I think that it's I don't think that it's a reconceptualization of the First Amendment. So the marketplace of ideas, the the entire way it sort of came into being and was used came up in the context of opinion um and not something that was a hard fact. >> So um there is no such thing as a false opinion. In other words, you I think that there should be more focus on immigration. I think there should be less focus on immigration. I think there should be more focus on fentinel. I think there should be less focus on fentinel. Those are all opinions. Or I think that the constitution um validly can be viewed as including a woman's right to choose. I disagree. I think that it doesn't include that. All of those are opinions and those are ones where you can you can completely imagine and I think correctly that there is a marketplace of ideas and and people have that discussion and we have a problem in terms of how that marketplace is functioning but nothing about what I am saying is in is restricting that in any way shape or form. I'm talking about something that's a cold hard fact. So one of the examples I give is um uh if there is gravity in the world that is not something that is subject to a marketplace of ideas or my father was a scientist and there are certain things that are cures there. There's objective data for that. Um and you're not going to subject to uh to the a marketplace of ideas something that is actually objectively verifiable and the Supreme Court has said that um so the Supreme Court has said that false statements in and of themselves are not protected. Now there's and there's various justices who have various embroideries on that but the idea that false statements themselves are protected um is something that um is now a truism. That's why Michael that you can have false statements to Congress are criminalized. False statements about election workers in Georgia are subject to civil laws. If you defame Egene Carol, you can be sued successfully and um and be required to pay tens of millions of dollars if you lie to um Congress and if you lie to banks, etc., etc., all of that. No one says, "Wait a second, the first amendment protects it." And so, um, my extension is about the question, why are we exempting politicians from that as opposed to saying, wait a second, isn't that a total reconceptualizing of the First Amendment? I would argue it's not. It's a question of what are the upsides and downsides of applying it in an in this other context. >> Okay. So, let me ask for the record. I agree with everything you're saying, but I wouldn't be doing my job if I didn't throw some provocations out there. >> Oh, wait. Totally. Totally love it. By the way, I should tell you I went to I did a bit of a book tour in in London >> and some of the best, smartest, most provocative questions I got were from uh various British uh interlocators. Um, and it's, you know, as as you as I know you appreciate, it makes it more fun and more >> 100%. All right. So, my first question is, are we at a point where we as a society agree enough on what a fact is full stop for this to be workable? And I'm going to be a little bit unfair and pull a somewhat obscure example. You mentioned gravity as being a fact, but we live in a world where less than a few decades ago, a professor was able to write an article in a journal called social text where he argued that gravity was a social construct. Um this was widely known as the soal affair after the author Alan Sul and his point was that even in a um peer-reviewed system there were ways to get through the gatekeepers and make something patently insane sound true. So you know the answer to that this is where um I would say the practical side of me comes in having you know just practiced law for so long. I think the answer to that is if the government couldn't prove that what the the statement is both false and intentionally so >> you're out of this doesn't apply and um what you're saying is um there may be issues factual issues where the person can say that's not even a false statement. So opinions of course are never going to be covered because they're not a false statement. And even if you could show that it's false, you'd have to show it's intentionally false. So in your example, you may be able to show and convince a jury, let's say beyond a reasonable doubt that it's false because there is gravity. Um but they then would have to assess whether it was intentionally so. Um and by the way, that's what we do now. Just think about defamation law where if you defame a public figure, it's it's not enough for the plaintiff to say that what they said about me is wrong. You have to show that it was said with actual malice, which is a a mental state that's required. >> Um and so that's all that would happen here. And so the more that the um the fact is not is not actually you know universally agreed on and um the the easier it would be um having been a defense lawyer um I would know this but you know this too just as having been an FBI agent because you think about what are the defenses going to be and so I think that's the answer to to that issue. >> Okay. Now I'm guessing you started writing this book some time ago if not an incredibly long time like books just date the ideas come you mentioned a conversation with your parents that sort of forms the seed for this one and in the interim between what I assume was the beginning of this project and now its publication we've seen a real uptick at least in the federal level of what I think most people would not have a problem saying are selective and vindictive prosecutions. >> Mhm. >> Do you have any concerns that promulgating this sort of regime now would give more easily reachable ammunition to those who do want to weaponize federal prosecutions. So I that is um I address that towards the end as sort of one of the key downsides is is this going to lie around like a check check off gun um that somebody can use >> which just for those in the audience who do not love Russian literature apparently the way we do Czechov's law is if you show a gun in act one it has to go off in act three and and it creates tension. Yes, >> because it's like there and you know you're sort of you know as an audience member you're like um you're sort of waiting for it. So um I I think uh so I'm not going to get I I actually address that in detail but let me just give you sort of the high level of where I am on that. Um there's so many weapons that are exist right now. It is true that this could be a potential new one, but I think that that when I'm assessing the pluses and minuses, I think it depends on where you think we are in this country. >> Um, and how much of a crisis you think we're in. Uh, and even for people who are not sold on this on my idea. Um or um I I just wrote something for the Times about these a way to quell vindictive prosecutions, which by the way I think you would like because it's I think it app it should actually appeal to everyone. But in any event, I think that if you think that we're in a a sort of fundamental crisis as opposed to just a pendulum swing and don't worry, people will learn and we won't get back here anytime soon. Um I I think that that will dictate the how you view this. But I do think no matter where you are in my idea, I think it's really important that we think about structures and you will like this phrase because I think it sort of goes to our upbringing at the FBI which is how do you harden the target? How do you make it, that's a phrase that is used in the intelligence community very much to say, how do you make um a a target you want to protect harder to infiltrate? Whether you're talking about cyber attacks, whether you're talking about buying by foreign adversaries, whether you're talking about terrorism, physical terrorism, how do you harden the target here, that being our democracy against authoritarian tactics and tendencies and people? >> Yeah, I think that makes sense. And I don't want to belabor the point because I think you and I are on the same page about where we are on that spectrum now and what level of alarm people if not emoting about should at least be considering in their own mind. Um but it's important to note I think while the problem may have reached an inflection point in the United States, this is as we've said before not a new concern and you are not the first thinker to write about it. Um, what you're talking about is the first argument I'm aware of to justify politicians or rulers lying to their people is the allegory of the of the cave in Plato, which for those who don't know, essentially imagine the polity is living in a cave with limited light and all they can see on the wall of the cave are shadows of the real world. It is the job of the ruler to be an intermediary and interpret those signs for the citizenry. Um they should not be allowed to have firsthand access to the actual truth outside of that sort of moderating force. to really reach for an analogy. You know, it's sort of um the difference between the early church and the justification by faith alone of Protestantism. Like should you be allowed to know the divine truth without intercession from an appointed body? You know, this is great. Do you know this is really I I studied in uh a college and on a fellowship after college uh 17th century uh theology >> um and very focused on sort of Puritanism, Catholicism, uh a ver a version of Protestantism that was aligned with Cartisianism. >> People are probably like going, "What the hell is going on?" Um but I I think just to bring it back to um this issue of the role of um political leaders and it goes to something we were just briefly chatting about before we started which is there is the Plato model. Um and one of the thinkers that I reference in my introduction which is >> and this is what I want to get to. Yeah. >> Yeah. Is my introduction by the way is extremely personal. like it is for people who like you know people who don't like me don't read the introduction but it's a very personal >> story which it's funny when I was on with Nicole Wallace I knew she would go right to that those issues because I never talk about that stuff on air and so I knew she would be like really fascinated by it but I talk about um uh Carl Pauper >> who was not a fan of Plato or at least in the in the sense in the area where which you have raised Um and in terms of the role of the citizen and the individual and what's owed to them and the role that they should play visav sort of the um the sort of political leaders. >> Yeah. And I mean that's sort of what I want to get to because I don't think it's a coincidence that popper came of intellectual age during the 30s and 40s um and was part of that intelligencia generation that really grew up um if I'm getting my if I'm not misremembering things like he was somebody who was very much part of the intellectual ferment that begins with the decline and fall of the AustroHungarian Empire, the Hapsburgs and leads to this just absolute flowering of intelle of you know social sciences and humanities and literature and art that comes to a crashing halt with the rise of fascism in Europe in the 30s. And that's the background against which he's writing. And I'm wondering if in alluding to him in your book, you are making an implicit comparison to a precipice we may be standing on now. >> Absolutely. That that is that is 100% what I was thinking about. I think I make that fairly clear when I talk about my father's history. He was born in 1930 in Vienna and my grandparents, his parents were part of that uh intelligencia. My grandfather, his father was a doctor at the university there and was very much in denial about what was going on and thinking it was it's a little bit like the way I think about New York now, which is, you know, couldn't happen here because, you know, look how sophisticated uh people are and cultured and also relatively tolerant as a community at the time. And uh as a result of that uh my grandparents did not leave Austria uh when they should have and instead they managed to leave in 1938 which for some people listening to this are going to go that's that's late because it was after the borders closed. And so um you know my I start the book with a conversation with my parents where my father directly alludes to that period in history when we are having this conversation about McCarthyism about Trump's first term um and he is relating what he his experience. So, I just want to interject with one thing, which is there are inevitably going to be people who hear this having not been familiar with Carl Pauper beforehand that are going to jump to the assumption he's some sort of creature of the left. So, I'm I'm going to point out that Carl Pauper has three targets in his work, the Open Society and His Enemies and its enemies, and the latter two are Hegel and Marx. This is not this is not somebody who is an ideologue for any side, but rather genuinely interested in freedom of thought and what allows for a flourishing society. I I kind of want to pursue this this sort of Carl Pauper thought by bringing in another thinker who you don't extensively talk about, but you do quote. Um, and that's Hannah Arent. And Hannah Arent and Carl Pauper both come out of the same sort of intellectual environment. But after the war, Hannah has a very different approach to those who enabled totalitarianism than a lot of other thinkers of her ilk. She writes extensively on it. Um her book uh the origins of totalitarianism is sort of a gold standard of sociology for authoritarianism and and its creation and ascension. Um I think a lot of people disagree with some of its assumptions and conclusions now but it's it's not a pmic. It's an actual academic work that attempts to figure out how did these things happen. But at the same time, she's remarkably forgiving of many of the people who enable it. I'm thinking in particular, she had an on-again, off-again love affair with Martin Haidiger. Um, I was going to make some pun about wearing his fascism on his sleeve, except he very much actually does. I mean, he's a member of the Nazi party who helps >> jettison Jewish intellectuals from the various universities. I think H Highleberg or Gertingen where he's working throughout the war. And I want to ask you as a veteran of the FBI and a veteran of the DOJ, there are certainly explicit bad actors who are enabling the weaponization of the legal system. There are the opposite. There are people who left or got fired out of a refusal to do so. But there's also a wide swath in between of careerists who maybe are trying to keep their head heads down, maybe are working on things that in and of themselves are unobjectionable or maybe just think that their work is apolitical and not connected. And I just want to get your point of view because it's something I've been puzzling over a lot um with many of my friends and former colleagues about if you believe that the Justice Department has been corrupted, what do we do once this is done? The the explicitly bad actors are an easy question, >> right? um what do we do or how do we approach careerists who allowed the machine to continue running but they themselves were not malifactors. I hope I'm articulating this in an understanding. >> Yeah. So, so to to be so let me give you my take and obviously again there's there's different categories. The people who are being actually being uh asked to do things that are illegal or unethical viol you know we've seen so many illegal is an easy category. unethical is the category of not obeying your duty to the court in terms of cander. And you know, we've seen examples of of that um that judges of all stripes have called out and and so that group um fall into the sort of complicity group. Um and uh I maybe unlike Hannah or Rent, I view um those those people as particularly um problematic because there are always going to be bad truly bad actors. You will have political leaders who are corrupt. Um Donald Trump in my view is not the first and he is not the last. Um there will I worked on the Enron case. The leaders of Enron were were convicted. But the story of Enron is not about them. The if you want the lesson of Enron is the complicity of those people who knew what was going on or closed their eyes to what was going on. And so within DOJ, those people may not have criminal liability, but I think that they um they do need to be um removed. Um and obviously I think there should be due process and I think they should be have a chance to be heard and if they you know if you can't show they did anything wrong then that's so be it. But I think for people who, let's just take my my office in the Eastern District of New York where I grew up. Um, you know, let's say you're dealing with somebody who's um been there for a year or two. They're doing their narcotics cases, which is sort of a typical sort of training ground, and it's all um business as usual in terms of what is in front of them. And those people are thinking, well, I wanted to be a prosecutor. I don't I'm not in any way doing anything wrong. I'm not a being asked to do anything wrong. I wouldn't if asked do something wrong. And um these people are I think um are who I'm prosecuting are these are righteous cases and and and I am modeling good behavior. Mhm. >> So, um I don't have a view that those people should leave now uh because they can't be part of that system. I think it's better for them to be there um for a whole variety of reasons and I don't think anything should happen to them later. And also there in many ways they're victims having to even think about this. It's like not sort of part of the job. In the same way, I want the same thing to be true with FBI agents and analysts and staff and there maybe even more so because of it the national security mission. I want to make sure that we have the very very best people there now um to keep people safe. It's that's sort of a paramount importance. Um I it's funny. I don't think the issue of like rebuilding DOJ, rebuilding the FBI. I don't see that as being the hardest issue. I think the FBI is going to be harder than DOJ and I'd be really interested in your view on this and the but I think DOJ is going to be relatively easy to build um its reputation back and to model it. And I think there's various things they can do. The FBI, I think, is harder because people go there for their career. And if you now have to think every four years, um, you know, we could have another Donald Trump, we could have no protection. um the old saying of um which and which is I think not just unique to the FBI which is um you know big cases big problems little cases little problems no cases no problems the idea is that if you keep your head down and don't do anything don't do anything or don't do anything controversial that's the way to have a long career I think that to me is the challenge for for institutions that rely on and need and just to be clear the public needs >> long-term expertise. >> Yeah. I think I've got two things in response to that. Um, I remember after the 2016 election, right around the time of the Muslim ban, I've always considered myself somebody on the center right, if not explicitly right, and a number of my friends very much on the left, asked me how I could stay in a government that was promulgating a policy like that. And my response was pretty simple, which is just because we have domestic disagreements right now does not mean that the Ministry of State Security from China or the Russian SVR are not doing everything they can to undermine US national security. And that's ultimately going to bounce back on the citizens, including the ones who voted against Trump. And I have a moral obligation to stay and try and stop them. Um but I but I think you hit on another point which a lot of people on the outside don't understand which is one can very one can very easily and very frequently as I believe you yourself have done leave and come back and leave and come back to the department of justice. It's >> not only easy it's something that's almost expected. There are very few people who start as a line a USA and spend their entire career at DOJ steadily rising the ranks. but is far more common. You do a couple years as an A USA. Maybe you become chief or deputy chief of a section within your US attorney's office. You go to the white shoe firm world for a couple years. Maybe you come back as a higher position at the US attorney's office or main justice. Um maybe you leave again and then maybe you come back as a political appointee. When I was in law school, I suspect it was the same as when you were in law school. That was actually sort of the dream progression of 75% if not more of the people who wanted to go into some sort of public service. The FBI is emphatically not like that. Uh by design people do not leave, get other experiences and come back. And you know I've been asked if there's a change of an administration would you go back to the FBI? And my answer is maybe maybe not. But I can't because the fact is under FBI policy, if you leave for more than two years and you want to come back, you have to go through the FBI academy again in its entirety and you have to start again as a line level investigator. I'm quite sure my wife and child would not allow me to disappear for 21 weeks. Um, I'm quite sure that my >> I think that Cash Patel, didn't Cash Patel cut it down? um >> prior law enforcement supposedly. Um I also don't think my body pushing 50 could take three days a week of hand-to-hand combat. But as a result of that sort of non-permeable border with the private sector, the FBI has a culture which is incredibly difficult to change. And my biggest worry is that we are going to have a core of agents who came in starting in January of 2025 up to um you know four years from now or sorry January of 2020 yeah 2025 and four years from now and they're going to be a cultured to an FBI that they have never known as apolitical and extrepating that kind of thought I think is going to be infinitely harder than people realize. >> Yeah. I I and I think I think that's true. I think what I said is true in terms of recruiting. I just think uh you know that's where going back to my theme of thinking about systemic issues, civil service rules are you know gravely gravely under attack. they don't even apply to the bureau. And the the need for us to think our way out of this problem and for people to understand like I know it doesn't sound very sexy to be like um you politicians need to run on the civil service, >> but it's true. I there's a reason for the civil service is that we lived in a system of the spoil system um for an incredibly long time. Uh and that is not healthy. Um that so there's just this is one where um it presupposes an engaged electorate and an engaged Congress um to deal with this. But to that to me is the only real way to solve this sort of FBI issue which by the way we're dealing with the FBI because we were both there but it's the same issue in many many other agencies where these are this is where people spend their career. Um and they had previously been trained to be completely apolitical. I mean, I remember Michael when I went from I'd worked for in the department for years and years and years and the first time I worked for Director Mueller was as his special counsel for 6 months and then I came back as the general counsel and it is it was so palpable how independent and fact-based the FBI was and how proud it was of that that it was in Washington, but it it just kept its head down on all of those issues. I mean, Director Mueller would be asked to weigh in on various policy issues or like a new law was being proposed um or to testify in support of some new administration uh law enforcement position and he was like that not doing it. I mean, his view was that the FB it was so important for the FBI to have integrity for everybody, Republican, Democrat, independent, so that they kept itself out of those debates. Yeah. I remember um midway through my career when I wasn't quite a senior executive. I also wasn't a field agent. I was what the FBI in its um overly complicated vocabulary would call a senior leader. um dealing with a particularly thorny counterintelligence problem that had a very easy legislative fix. We weren't going to propose it. It it just it wasn't viewed as our place. There is a political appointee at the Department of Justice. Department of Justice has an office of legislative affairs and if the administration wants to speak, it will be through people who do not carry guns. There was just there was something deeply ingrained in the American spirit that if you had the power to use force against the public in certain circumstances, you had an obligation to be a sort of greyman. Don't wear your feelings or your ideologies on your sleeve. You have to be the epitome of apolitical so that when you do have to do something extreme to protect other people, nobody's going to second guessess your judgments. Um, and I and the loss of that trust is something that does still keep you up at night. To me that is, you know, when you said that you and I probably have exactly the same view about where we are on the spectrum of, you know, K on fire versus pendulum swinging, you know, the the story after story of what is happening in the judicial system is so abnormal. um under and both of us have worked in you know for different administrations and it's just it just isn't anything that happens. You also you don't see mass firings or even or people quitting over being asked to do things that are improper um in in any administration other than now. >> Yeah, this is the Saturday massacre except it's lasting 18 months >> um with no signs of slowing down. And >> I think on that slightly pessimistic note um being respectful of your time, we will leave things. Um, but I I would unhesitatingly recommend your book for anybody who wants to try and understand what solutions to the problem of fraud in politics, by which I mean colloquially speaking, lying in politics might be out there that haven't previously been on the table in this country. Um, and I think the set of circumstances we're in right now is sort of exactly the right moment to start examining those options. Well, thank you so much for having me. Great discussion. Thanks for coming here.

transcript_segments.text: We are here today with Andrew Weissman
who is an accomplished professor of law

for the purposes of this podcast has
also occupied a wide slate of positions
at both the main justice department and
the FBI. He worked in the US attorney's
office for the Eastern District of New
York. He worked at main justice in the
fraud section and he was also the
general counsel of the FBI. So he brings
a wide variety of experience to the
topics we are going to discuss today. I
want to open our conversation
with a sort of inquiry
about why this book is coming out now.
And the answer is a little bit
self-evident from the title. But the
problem of politicians lying to the
public is really quite evergreen. I
think if we go back to Homer, we would
find examples of it there. And it's
certainly something that any historian
of the LA latter-day United States would
be familiar with. So why is this moment
of deceit in politics different from
those that have occurred in the past?

know, when I was at the bureau uh and
was the general counsel, I used to
religiously
read lawfare just like every single day
because they just had such a great um
compilation of people and issues and it
was really smart and um I think it's
done a great job of speaking to multiple
audiences um both people sort of in the
field and then also lay people and if
anything it's gotten better um and wider
in terms of what it does. So any that's
a long way of saying thank you so much
um for having me um and it's great
having this conversation with you um
given our FBI overlap um in in terms of
our our work experience. Um so uh so uh
so the the book is Liars's Kingdom and
so as you're you're correct uh and I
point out in the book that um this is
not a unique time. It's not like we
don't have substantial
historical
uh examples that we can point to. Um and
you know there's obviously Mark Twain
you know who commented um you know very
famously and quipped about um political
lies. And so on the one hand I do think
it is a perennial problem and it's one
that I think other times in our history
and other countries and civilizations
have had to deal with. Having said that,
um I don't think in my lifetime
that we've had such
brazeness about lies and ones that are
so readily accepted by an inordinate
uh number of people. So, let me just
explain really quickly what I mean by
that, which is so one example of, you
know, a lie that that I sort of take
throughout this book is the big lie
about the 2020 election that there was
material fraud. Um, I obviously can't
cover every every lie told by Donald
Trump or every politician. The
Washington Post catalog just in the
first term that there were 30,000 false
or misleading statements. So I didn't
want to write a encyclopedia. I wanted
to write something that people could

polling on that which has been
incredibly consistent are that twothirds
of Republicans believe that and a third
of the electorate believes that lie. And
I think part of that is because we are
in a substantially different media
environment than we have been in
certainly since um Oliver Wendell Holmes
talked about sort of the marketplace of
ideas. I mean he didn't actually use
that exact phrase but that was sort of
his concept. Um, and because of this
bulcanization of the media, I think that
lies can be much more pernitious and
prevalent because the whole concept that
they would rub up against the truth and
be combed by the truth and justice is
saying um, you know, the remedy for
false speech is true speech. That
presupposes that they're going to be
heard. Um, and with our current media
environment, which I don't see getting
any better, that precondition for a
marketplace of ideas is no longer there
in the way that it used to be.
>> Okay. So, you mentioned Wendell Holmes's
famous well his statements which later

ideas.
>> Exactly. And that was in the context of

And your book is very much a dialogue,

is going to get some push back from

>> Yeah. So, um, maybe just turning to sort
